You can use various strategies to meet procurement needs effectively, ranging from specific contracts to broader procurement tools that serve multiple departments and agencies. The choice of method of supply depends on the clarity of initial requirements.

There are three methods of supply for procuring goods and services:

contracts (including those with task authorizations [TA])

standing offers

supply arrangements

You must select the method of supply in consultation with the client, focusing on what best meets their needs. A comparison table is included below to help decide when the precise nature, quantity or timing cannot be set out in advance.

Existing procurement instruments

Before selecting a new method of supply, you must first verify if the required goods or services are available through an existing procurement instrument. Advise clients to use existing mandatory or non-mandatory standing offers and supply arrangements whenever possible.

Mandatory standing offers and supply arrangements

PSPC has put in place a number of standing offers and supply arrangements that must be used before any new procurement is considered, per the Treasury Board's (TB) Directive on the Management of Procurement .

Mandatory product and service categories

N84: Clothing, Individual Equipment and Insignia

N58: Communication, Detection, and Coherent Radiation Equipment

N91: Fuels, Lubricants, Oils and Waxes

N71: Furniture

N70: General Purpose Automatic Data Processing Equipment (Including Firmware), Software, Supplies and Support Equipment

N23: Ground Effect Vehicles, Motor Vehicles, Trailers and Cycles

D3: Information Processing and Related Telecommunication Services

N74: Office Machines, Text Processing Systems and Visible Record Equipment

N75: Office Supplies and Devices

R: Professional, Administrative and Management Support Services

RO: Professional Services

R1: Administrative and Management Support Services

R2: Personnel Recruitment

Departments and agencies must verify whether a mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement exists that can meet their requirements. If one does, they must use it. This applies to all departments and agencies, as defined in Section 2 of the Financial Administration Act , including the Canadian Forces. You should point out to clients that using mandatory procurement instruments typically offer faster acquisition and reduced administrative costs.

Key procedural elements

Mandatory procurement instruments, such as standing offers and supply arrangements, must not be used when one of the following applies:

the good or service available does not meet justifiable operational requirements, including specifications or delivery dates

the value of the requirement exceeds the call-up limitation of the standing offer or the scope of the supply arrangement

an existing contract is in place, which guarantees the work to another supplier

the requirement falls under Comprehensive Land Claims Agreement(s) (CLCA) contracting obligations, and no mandatory standing offer/supply arrangement exists that addresses the contracting addresses these obligations

Clients must justify why they deviated from using an available standing offer or supply arrangement. You must escalate any disputes you may have with the client concerning the application of mandatory instruments to the appropriate manager or director responsible for that standing offer or supply arrangement. If the client disagrees with the decision, they can escalate the matter to their Acquisitions Account manager in Procurement Assistance Canada . Ensure that you record all instances where a mandatory instrument was not used and include the reasons.

Clients are not permitted to establish their own standing offers or supply arrangements to avoid using PSPC standing offers or supply arrangements, as this is the intent to achieve government-wide benefits and savings.

If a client identifies a lower cost for an equivalent good or service by means other than the mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement, they must report this to you. In these cases, you must consider adjustments to the existing arrangements.

Comparing different methods of supply

The table below outlines the differences between the methods of supply when the precise nature, timing or quantity of a requirement cannot be established in advance.

Guidelines for selecting the appropriate method of supply

Methods of supply: Determining the Method to Use | Contract with TA: Use in service contracting situations when: the client requires quick access to one or more category of service(s) expected to be needed on a repetitive basis all or a portion the work can be defined but the exact nature and timeframes of the required services, activities and deliverables will only be known as and when the service(s) will be required Conditions for issuing TAs must be explicitly stated in the contract. A TA facilitates work authorization under the contract terms on an "as and when requested" basis. TAs are not individual contracts. | Standing offer: Appropriate when: requirements are clearly defined but expected quantities or level of effort and timing are not known there are no minimum work guaranteed multiple sources of supply is preferred for commonly needed, commercially available goods or services A call-up against a standing offer is a contract. | Supply arrangement: Used when recurring needs for a certain type of good or service exist, but a standing offer is not suitable due to the inability to fully define the requirement. The supply arrangement establishes a pool of suppliers to compete for the requirement under a predefined framework.

Methods of supply: Solicitation Process and Evaluation Criteria | Contract with TA: A solicitation of offers is used to select offerors to establish contracts that will allow services to be carried out on an "as and when requested basis." | Standing offer: A Request for Standing Offer (RFSO) is used to select offerors, who meet evaluation criteria to provide goods or services or both at predetermined pricing under set conditions. The RFSO provides approximate quantities of goods or the level of effort for services and estimated costs. | Supply arrangement: A Request for Supply Arrangement (RFSA) is used to establish a source list of pre-qualified suppliers who meet the solicitation’s evaluation criteria, helping quick solicitation of offers for specific requirements within the scope of an supply arrangement .

Methods of supply: Contractual obligation | Contract with TA: The contract creates a contractual obligation with funding committed to cover the total estimated cost of the contract limited by minimum guarantee clauses or other contract considerations. | Standing offer: There is no contractual obligation on the part of Canada until a call-up is made. Each call-up is a separate contract with funds committed at that time. The standing offer is not a contract. The offeror is bound by its offer unless its offer is withdrawn. | Supply arrangement: There is no contractual obligation between Canada and the offeror to buy goods or services at the supply arrangement stage. There is a contractual obligation with the award of each contract.

Methods of supply: Binding Legal Agreement | Contract with TA: The signed contract is the binding legal agreement. TAs are administrative tools for task execution under the contract. | Standing offer: The call-up is the acceptance of the offer and is a binding contract for that specific procurement. | Supply arrangement: Each contract awarded under the supply arrangement will be considered to be a separate binding contract.

Methods of supply: Work Authorization | Contract with TA: The work is authorized as needed when a TA is authorized by the client or by PSPC . | Standing offer: Work is authorized on an "if and when requested" basis with each issued call-up. | Supply arrangement: Work is authorized through individual contracts issued under the supply arrangement.

Methods of supply: Financial Limits | Contract with TA: The limit for the client for authorizing a TA is set in the contract. PSPC can issue TAs exceeding the client’s limit. | Standing offer: The call-up limit is set in the standing offer document for the identified user(s), adhering to contracting approvals of the Directive on the Management of Procurement. | Supply arrangement: Contracting limits are set in the supply arrangement for the client or identified user(s), adhering to contracting approvals of the Directive on the Management of Procurement .

Methods of supply: Minimum Guarantee or Other Consideration | Contract with TA: When TAs are used exclusively, a minimum supply commitment is required, and the contract must limit the contractor's expectations. | Standing offer: Not applicable. | Supply arrangement: Not applicable.

Methods of supply: Payment Provisions | Contract with TA: The payment terms are firm: basis of payment; method of payment; all inclusive firm rates, etc. | Standing offer: The payment terms are firm: basis of payment; method of payment; all inclusive firm rates, etc. | Supply arrangement: Basis of payments with ceiling prices or rates are established, or sometimes no rates are provided until individual requirements are competed. Ceiling prices or rates allow the opportunity to offerors to lower their prices or rates based on the actual requirement detailed in the solicitation of offers.

Methods of supply: Monitoring and Reporting | Contract with TA: Monitoring and reporting of contractor performance and client usage of TAs are mandatory. | Standing offer: Monitoring and reporting of contractor performance and client usage are required as per the terms of the standing offer. | Supply arrangement: Monitoring and reporting of contractor performance and client usage are required as per the terms of the supply arrangement.

Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements and methods of supply

When selecting a method of supply, such as standing offers, supply arrangements, or contracts, you must determine, in consultation with clients, whether it needs to meet CLCA requirements. This includes analyzing past, present and future needs to develop mutually beneficial solutions for PSPC and its clients.

You have various options for addressing CLCA s, such as:

a single instrument covering both CLCA and non- CLCA areas

separate streams of instruments, including one dedicated to CLCA areas and another for non- CLCA areas

multiple streams addressing various geographic or CLCA -specific areas

Addressing CLCA obligations

If an instrument involves CLCA s, you must address the applicable CLCA procurement obligations at the solicitation stage. For supply arrangements, these obligations must also be addressed when issuing any solicitation of offers under the supply arrangements that may be subject to CLCA s.

Planning for non- CLCA provisions

If you determined that no or few resulting contracts or TAs will be subject to CLCA s, then it may be more appropriate to issue an instrument that excludes CLCA s provisions. In this case, you must include a clause in the instrument stating that deliveries and services cannot occur within CLCA settlement areas under any resulting contract or task.

If a future client’s requirement arises that is subject to CLCA s, and no instrument exists which meets the applicable CLCA s obligations, the requirement must be handled as a new procurement.

Exemptions and mandatory instruments

If a client's requirement is subject to CLCA contracting obligations, and no mandatory standing offers or supply arrangements exists which addresses the obligations, the client department is exempted from using the mandatory instrument.

Contracts

You can use contracts for goods, services and construction, to meet unique, well-defined requirements for a single or multiple clients. They are used where a standing offer or supply arrangement is not warranted.

Contracts with Task Authorizations

A contract with TAs is a flexible method of supply for services, under which all or a portion of the work will be performed on an “as and when requested” basis. These contracts are used in scenarios where the client anticipates needing rapid access to services, throughout the contract period. A TA is not an individual contract.

Key aspects of contracts with TAs include:

predetermined conditions establishing the framework for issuing TAs upfront

defined need for services, making these contracts optimal for foreseeable, repetitive service needs

administrative process structuring the TAs as mechanisms for authorizing work within the contract's terms

Application

You may use contracts with TAs for service requirements such as:

professional services

informatics professional services

technical investigation and engineering studies

some types of repair and overhaul (R&O) services where work authorizations are considered TAs , for example:

Additional work requirements

Mobile repair party

Special investigations and technical studies

Project work order

Exclusions

You must not use contracts with TAs for shipbuilding, ship refits or for R&O work authorizations not classified as TAs . These include Selection Notice and Priority Summary, Repairable Materiel Request, Quartermaster Transfer Orders and Vehicle Movement Orders.

Guidelines for incidental goods

TAs may be used for incidental goods procurement related to a specific service requirement when these goods will become Canada’s property. You must clearly outline conditions for incidental acquisition of goods in approval documents, solicitations and contracts.

TAs must not ease the acquisition of goods by the contractor on behalf of the client outside the scope of work to bypass the normal procurement process. For instance, a contract with TAs for engineering services cannot be used to purchase software unless this requirement was part of the original solicitation and related payment provisions of the contract.

Oversight

Directors have the authority to approve or prohibit classes of procurement in which TAs may be used.

Conditions of use

Proper application of contracts with TAs is critical to avoid significant issues between Canada and its offerors, between PSPC and its clients, and for the government in the eyes of the public. When considering a contract with TAs as a method of supply, you should consider certain contract conditions, this includes:

ensuring that the contract with TAs is properly assessed as the most suitable method of supply for the intended services

determining the client’s ability to issue TAs and establish any financial limitations

providing the client with the guidelines for preparing and administering TAs , ensuring they understand their purpose and application

establishing an agreement with the client that clearly outlines the roles and responsibilities of both parties, emphasizing the importance of reporting, and ensuring the timely exchange of all relevant documentation, including:

copies of all TAs and their attachments

copies of all revisions to TAs with their attachments

copies of all claims/invoices, supported by detailed reports

ensuring the process is clear by discussing the use of contracts with TAs with the client, as required, focusing on the administrative process for authorizing and issuing

ensure clients provide completed resources assessment worksheets that demonstrate the proposed resources meet the requirements for the applicable category and level when requesting PSPC authorization of TA forms; you must keep this information on the procurement file

ensure clients provide proof of verified security requirements, including a copy of the security clearance confirmation for each proposed resource, when requesting PSPC authorization of TA forms; you must keep this information on the procurement file

Solicitation of offers and resulting contract documents

You must ensure that solicitation of offers and resulting contractual documents for establishing a contract with TAs follow the standard approach.

For the work to be performed on an “as and when requested” basis, you must include specific elements in the solicitation and contractual documents, to ensure clarity, compliance and efficiency, such as:

a clear statement of work broadly describing the scope of work to be performed under the TAs

a detailed description of the administrative process for authorizing and issuing TAs , including all relevant procurement clauses. For contracts involving multiple TAs , the solicitation must explain how contractors will be ranked and how work will be allocated, which will be finalized at the time of contract award

the TA limits for authorizing and using TAs by the client in accordance with guidance in the section below, Setting financial limits on individual TAs

the identification and provision of the applicable task authorization form (PDF, 161 KB, accessible only on the Government of Canada network) for authorizing and issuing TAs

the payment provisions applicable to the TA , such as basis or bases of payment and method of payment

Obligation and total liability

Full contract through TAs : When all work under a contract is to be performed through TAs you must ensure to include applicable procurement clauses in the contract, such as clauses that:

limit Canada's obligation for TAs and to provide a minimum work guarantee for the contractor

limit Canada’s total liability under the contract for all approved TAs , when it is subject to a Limitation of Expenditure and the contract cover page must reflect the total estimated cost equal the stipulated limitation of expenditure.

Partial work through TAs : When a portion of the work is to be performed through TAs it is essential for you to ensure that applicable procurement clauses are included to limit Canada’s total liability under the contract. The "Total estimated cost" shown on the cover page of the contract document must equal the total of the limitation of expenditure stipulated under the clause.

Monitoring and reporting : You are responsible to monitor TA usage and should include a provision for reporting requirements by the contractor on TA usage.

Setting financial limits on individual task authorizations

Financial limits for TAs are crucial for maintaining control over procurement activities and ensuring alignment with the contract’s scope and budget.

These limits are determined as follows.

TA authorization: TAs may be authorized and issued by both the client and PSPC . PSPC contracting authorities, who have approval and signing authority for service contracts, have no maximum dollar limit for authorizing individual TAs . Their authorization extends up to the total approved contract value, unless specified otherwise by management.

Client authorization: The decision to allow a client to issue TAs is determined by you, in consultation with the client. If clients are allowed to authorize TAs , you must establish a maximum limit on the dollar amount of a TA inclusive of applicable taxes.

Considerations: In establishing limits for individual TAs and any revisions to those TAs , you should balance operational effectiveness and contract risk management.

Factors to consider include:

whether an agreement has been reached between the client and PSPC on the conditions of use of TAs

the client and industry’s past performance history relative to the effective use of contracts with TAs , as experienced by the PSPC contracting authority, colleagues and managers

the client's procurement and contract management capabilities, including the availability and necessity of training and whether department policies and procedures are established and enforced

operational requirements, such as time sensitivity and other operational requirements of tasks

contract scope and risk, including the clarity and level of detail in the contract’s statement of work, task complexity and duration, price ease, average TA value and anticipated task frequency

comparison of work value not performed through TAs versus work done through TAs

how often TA usage is reported

TA thresholds

Note on TA thresholds: When determining the threshold for the contracting authority’s authorization of individual TAs , it's imperative that this level of oversight brings added value to the process without compromising control. This added value can take various forms, including financial oversight and comparisons with similar contracts to ensure value for money. The establishment of the client's TA limit should be informed by the department's experience in TA administration potentially setting a limit that ensures the contracting authority oversees a representative portion of TAs .

Documentation

You must keep a detailed and current record of all authorized tasks for each contract with TAs . The client or contractor is responsible for creating and updating this record, as established in consultation with the client during the procurement planning stage.

For each TA , this record may include:

the task number and any task revision numbers

a title or brief description

the total estimated cost specified in the TA (exclusive of taxes)

the amount expended to date (exclusive of taxes)

start and completion dates

the current status

On a cumulative basis, it should include:

the amount specified in the contract (as last amended) as Canada's total liability to the contractor for all authorized TAs (exclusive of taxes)

the amount expended to date against all authorized TAs (exclusive of taxes)

Forms

Use the form PWGSC- TPSGC 572 Task Authorization (accessible only on the Government of Canada network), for the authorization of tasks by both PSPC and clients other than the Department of National Defence (DND). Alternatively, any locally developed and approved TA forms may be used for non- DND contracts. The form DND 626 Task Authorization must be used in contracts for DND .

Using a standing offer

A standing offer is from an offeror to Canada that permits Canada to acquire goods and services. It is used when requested over a specified time frame, using a call-up process that includes pre-determined conditions and pricing. A standing offer is not a contract. A contract is established with each call-up for goods or services made against the standing offer Canada's liability obligation is limited to the actual value of call-ups made within the standing offer's validity period.

You can use the standing offer method of supply to meet client’s requirements for a specified period, especially when precise quantities for the period are uncertain. Standing offer’s may allow one or more authorized users to directly issue call-ups to offerors, up to specified maximum limits. PSPC may issue call-ups exceeding these limits, if stated in the standing offer.

A standing offer may be valid for one or more years depending on the specific product or service category. The quantity of goods and level of services specified in the request for standing offers ( RFSO ) and the resulting standing offer(s) are only an approximation of the requirements, provided in good faith by Canada to the offerors.

All government contracting policies, regulations and procedures, including trade agreement obligations, apply to standing offer. You must consider the estimated total value of all call-ups, including taxes, to determine the applicability of trade agreement procedures. If you plan to issue multiple standing offers against a single RFSO , then you must use the combined estimated value of all standings offers, including applicable taxes to obtain approval.

You are advised to consider a standing offer where appropriate taking into account their potential benefits and drawbacks. Benefits can include, improved client service and risk mitigation of overestimating. Drawbacks may involve, possible withdrawal by the offeror and potentially less competitive prices.

Standing offers can be competitive or non-competitive. You may direct a standing offer on a non-competitive basis to a sole offeror for its full range of catalogue goods or services, in which case non-competitive approval authorities would apply.

Standing offers are suited for situations where:

repetitive orders of the same goods or services are expected without precise details known in advance

the goods, services or both are well defined

conditions allow for pre-arranged prices or pricing bases, established upfront, usually through competition, without the need for negotiation at call-up

goods and services are readily available and can be ordered as needed without further negotiation of terms

Standing offers are not suited for situations where:

prices, pricing bases, or conditions are undefined or may change at any time at the offeror’s discretion

further negotiation of the standing offer terms is intended

a solicitation process is intended each time goods or services are required

Clients authorized by PSPC can make call-ups as defined in the standing offer. If trade agreement procedures apply, the entire procurement process, including authorized standing offers and call-ups, falls under the Canadian International Trade Tribunal's purview.

Security requirements, if any, within standing offers and call-ups need careful consideration. You must consider how the security requirements will be managed and ensure that a call-up issued against a standing offer meets the appropriate security requirements. You must inform client’s of their responsibility to confirm the security status of the organization or personnel before issuing the call-up.

If you have not sought or been given advance approval to issue a standing offer, then approval to issue each resulting standing offer is required. You must use the total estimated value of each resulting standing offer to determine the approval authority.

If advance approval for PSPC to issue call-ups on behalf of a client against a standing offer was not requested at the time of obtaining approval to establish the standing offer, you must use the procurement risk assessment to determine the approval authority for each call-up.

Ranking and methodology for multiple standing offers

For a procurement with multiple standing offers, where no single offeror could meet all demands, you must detail the ranking methodology and call-up procedure, in the RFSO . The following outlines the different methods involved.

Right of first refusal: This method involves contacting the highest-ranked offeror for each requirement. If they cannot satisfy the requirement, the process continues to the next ranked offeror and so on. This ensures a competitive process, until an offeror confirms the ability to fulfill the requirement. Call-ups are made based on the “right of first refusal.”

Proportional basis: This method assigns a predetermined portion of work to offerors based on their rank. The highest-ranked offeror receives the largest predetermined portion of the work; the second highest ranked offeror receives the second larges predetermined portion of the work, etc. For instance, the highest-ranked may receive 50% of the work, the next 30%, and the third 20%. You must clearly state this distribution in the RFSO to maintain transparency and competitiveness. This method is also known as "collective best value." The highest-ranked standing offer represents the best value for Canada, and its offeror receives the largest portion of the work.

Both methods are competitive, and you should clearly state the chosen ranking method in the RFSO . Include any specific requirements such as a breakdown of work distribution. In both cases you should clearly state in the RFSO the expected number of standing offers that is intended to be authorized for use.

When the intention is that multiple standing offers will be authorized for use, you should state in the RFSO the basis upon which call-ups will be issued. If a proportional basis is used, the breakdown must be stated. You could also include a condition that only standing offers within a percentage of the best-priced offer, for example 10%, will be considered. Be sure to clearly describe such method in the RFSO .

Non-competitive call-ups

In cases where more than one standing offer is authorized without a ranking system, such as when sourcing a wide range of catalog items, the authorized call-up authority can select any available standing offer. You should set parameters to guide the selection process, ensuring fairness even in non-competitive call-ups.

Contract Security Program

For security requirements that are contract specific, validation from the Contract Security Program (CSP) is essential. Examples of specific security requirements include document safeguarding, information technology (including cloud computing), productions or communication-electronic security (COMSEC). You must send all relevant call-ups to the CSP .

Limitation of expenditure

Including a limitation of expenditure in standing offers is optional. The approval authority to establish such offers will be determined by the results of the procurement risk assessment using “standing offer” delegation. When assessing risk, you must consider the total estimated procurement value for the entire standing offer period.

For standing offer with a limitation of expenditure, you must seek approval to revise its method of supply, according to the appropriate contract amendment delegation of authority for the specific commodity (goods, services).
